meta.description: Character × session/act beat matrix for At the Mountains of Madness

beats.notes: Referenced as designer of the boring apparatus
Player co-creation — Pabodie available as PC choice
Evaluates technical aspects of the briefing
Departs — motivated by unexplained core samples
Maintains drilling equipment. Notes instruments behaving oddly
Triangular markings in wrong strata. Equipment fails near certain sites
Finds Lake's private journal. Recognizes his own drill marks on destroyed equipment
Instruments fail completely. The mountains exceed all expectations
Engineering mind struggles with impossible architecture
Warm air from below. Temperature readings impossible for Antarctica
Recognizes tool marks in the tunnel walls — not geological, engineered
The empathy turn — headless Elder Things killed by something worse
Witnesses the Shoggoth. Everything rational shatters
Pure flight. Back through tunnels and city to surface
Engine sounds wrong. Looks back — the city extends endlessly
The clouds above the violet mountains. Fragments of something worse
The ship. The radio. The world wants an explanation
The Choice. His engineering mind wants to document. His survival instinct says burn it all
Referenced as marine biologist recruited by Moore
Available as PC choice — first woman on Antarctic geological survey
Notes biological claims in the briefing that defy classification
Departs — intends to classify what Dyer could not
Notes dogs' behavior changes as biological anomaly
The triangular markings contain biological material that shouldn't exist
The surviving specimen defies every taxonomy she knows
Scientific rigor demands investigation. Self-preservation says stop
The murals showing Shoggoth creation rewrites the definition of life
Underground ecosystem implies life forms adapted to conditions she cannot model
Giant blind albino penguins — an underground ecosystem proven real
Headless Elder Things. They were scientists too. 'Whatever they had been, they were men'
The Shoggoth. Life that has outgrown its creators
Escape. Carries biological samples she wishes she didn't have
The crossing back. She cannot stop cataloguing what she saw
The vision above the violet mountains
The world wants an explanation. Moore wants data
The Choice. Her samples are proof. Publishing rewrites biology. But also invites a third expedition
Not yet introduced
Available as PC choice — hired security, Gallipoli veteran
Evaluates the briefing for tactical concerns
Departs — the contract is simple: keep them alive
Notes dogs' distress. Checks perimeter out of habit
Night watch — hears sounds the wind doesn't explain
Reads the destruction at Lake's camp as combat, not accident
Scans terrain for threats. The scale offers no cover, no retreat
Treats the city as hostile territory. Marks exit routes
Advises against going deeper. Overruled or convinced
Takes point. Every tunnel is Gallipoli again but worse
Recognizes the Elder Things' deaths as combat losses. Soldiers' deaths
The Shoggoth. Cannot fight it. Cannot flank it. Can only run
Leads the retreat. This is what he was hired for
Kept them alive. The contract is fulfilled. But at what cost
Stares straight ahead during the vision. Refuses to look
Says nothing to anyone. Waits for the ship
The Choice. A soldier's perspective — some things shouldn't be known
Not yet introduced
Available as PC choice — cheap reporter, signed the waiver
Notes what's missing from the briefing. Already looking for the story
Departs — front-page stories don't bury themselves
Photographs the mirages. Interviews crew about previous voyages
Documents everything. This is bigger than the Innsmouth story
Photographs Lake's camp. Reads his journal. The story of a lifetime
Insists on going further. No story without the payoff
Photographs the murals. Fills three notebooks. No editor will believe this
The story behind the story. Warm air = something still alive down there
Still photographing. The flash disturbs the blind penguins
The headless Elder Things. This is no longer a story she wants to tell
Drops the camera. Doesn't go back for it
Runs. The notebooks are still in her pocket. The camera is not
Stares at her notebooks. Words but no photographs
The vision. She writes it down automatically. Reporter's instinct
The Gazette wants a story. She has one. But should she file it?
The Choice. Publish = fame + third expedition. Burn the notebooks = safety + obscurity. Dyer tried publishing. Nobody believed him
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — aircraft mechanic, keeps the Fairchilds flying
Half-listens to briefing. Already thinking about cold-weather engine mods
Departs — the machines need him
Preps the planes for Antarctic conditions. First odd readings
Equipment failures near certain sites. Nothing he fixes stays fixed
Recognizes deliberate damage to Lake's equipment. Not weather — intent
Calculates fuel margins. Crossing means limited return options
The architecture has no joints, no mortar. His mechanical mind can't process it
Warm air from below. Thermodynamically impossible
Nothing to fix down here. Nothing has parts. Out of his element
The Elder Things' tools are recognizable. They were builders, like him
The Shoggoth has no parts. Cannot be fixed, cannot be understood
Runs for the plane. The plane he can understand. The plane he can fix
In his element again. Engine sounds wrong but he nurses it through
Keeps eyes on gauges during the vision. Won't look out the window
Lands the plane. That's his job done. The rest is someone else's problem
The Choice. Machines don't lie. What he saw was real. But who'd believe a mechanic?
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — radio operator, lighthouse kid
Listens carefully. Taps Morse on his knee
Departs — as long as the radio works, they're not alone
Radio clear. Regular check-ins with Moore. First hints of unusual static
Static patterns that don't match any known interference. The radio 'breathes'
Radio reception degrades near Lake's camp. Something interferes
Radio contact with Moore fading. The wind sounds like it's trying to form words
Radio dead in the city. Silence for the first time. They are alone
No signal. No connection. The link to civilization is severed
Carries the radio out of habit. It receives nothing but the nameless sound
Silence meant danger in the lighthouse. This silence is worse
The Shoggoth's sound drowns everything. The anti-signal
Runs. Drops the radio. Doesn't go back
Without the radio, he is adrift. The wind still sounds like words
The vision. He hears something on a frequency that doesn't exist
The ship has a working radio. He can hear Moore again. But is it really Moore?
The Choice. The silence was the worst part. Telling someone means the silence might end
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — Starkweather's money man
Assesses cost-benefit of every claim in the briefing
Departs — protecting Starkweather's investment
Tracks expenses. Questions delays. Time is money
The anomalies have commercial implications. Begins second ledger
A specimen that defies classification is worth more than the entire expedition
Pushes to continue. The discovery value justifies any fuel risk
An alien city. The discovery of the millennium. Starkweather was right
Value vs. risk calculation breaks down. What's down there may be worth more — or worthless
Still calculating. Artifacts = profit. But who's the buyer for alien technology?
The ledger stops making sense when the entities are people
No commercial value. Only annihilation. The second ledger closes
Runs. Leaves the second ledger behind
Silent. For once, not calculating
Doesn't look at the vision. Stares at his hands
Old habits return. Starkweather will want a report. The investment...
The Choice. Tell Starkweather the truth = third expedition. Lie = protect the world but betray his employer
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — Moore's scientific observer, coded radio
Notes every unsupported claim. Reports to Moore via coded frequency
Departs — the science must be clean
Regular coded reports to Moore. Notes anomalies objectively
Coded report to Moore on the markings. Moore demands caution
Tries to report to Moore. Radio increasingly unreliable. Evidence is undeniable
Moore's last clear message: 'Do not cross the mountains.' Radio dies
Documents everything with geological rigor. The evidence is real. Moore must know
No way to report. Alone with evidence that rewrites geology
Takes samples. Labels everything. The discipline holds
Rigor will not be enough to contain what this means
The Shoggoth. No geological framework for this
Runs. The samples are in her pocket. The codebook is irrelevant now
Writes a report no one will read on a frequency no one monitors
The vision. Science has no vocabulary for this
Moore is on the radio. Demanding data. She has the data. All of it
The Choice. Send the data to Moore = enables third expedition. Destroy the samples = betray science. She was sent to keep it honest
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — Norwegian ice pilot, 20 years Antarctic waters
Knows the ice. Smells trouble in the briefing. Old sailors' stories
Departs — the ice is the only country he has left
The ice is wrong. Mirages persist too long. The dogs know
Recalls Lars Larsen's crew seeing stone ruins from a crow's nest in 1929
Reads the ice around Lake's camp. Something melted it from below
Warns against crossing. The old stories said these mountains are forbidden
The old stories were true. He always knew. Wishes he hadn't been right
Refuses to go below. May be convinced or overruled
If he descends: muttering in Norwegian. The old prayers
The ice knows. It always knew. These things lived under his ice for twenty years
The Shoggoth. The thing under the ice. The thing the old stories warned about
Leads through the city. Knows the way out by instinct
Stares at the ice below. It will never look the same
The violet mountains. He looks. He's seen enough that one more horror doesn't matter
Back on water. Says nothing. Smokes his pipe
The Choice. The ice should keep its secrets. It's done so for millions of years. Leave it alone
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — dog handler, Byrd expedition veteran
Dogs from the 1930 expedition were never right again. He wants to know why
Departs — the dogs came back wrong, and he intends to find out why
The dogs are uneasy. Dismissed as weather — but McReady knows dogs
Dogs refuse food. Won't face south. They know something is there
No dog remains at Lake's camp. The dogs either fled or were taken
Dogs refuse to approach the mountains. McReady trusts them over orders
No dogs in the city. Left them at the plane. They wouldn't enter
The dogs knew. They always know first
Moves like his dogs — low, cautious, sniffing the air
Now he knows why the 1930 dogs were never right again. They saw this
The Shoggoth. This is what the dogs smelled. This is what they refused to face
Runs like the dogs would. Straight for the exit. No looking back
Back at the plane. The dogs are frantic but alive. They fly
Holds one of the dogs during the vision. The dog whimpers
Takes care of the dogs. They're all that matters now
The Choice. The ice doesn't lie. But people do. Keep it simple — say nothing
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — already partially broken by what he believes is coming
The briefing confirms correlations he found in the texts
Departs — if the texts and Dyer describe the same thing, the pattern can be understood
The mirages match descriptions in the Pnakotic Manuscripts. The wind piping matches too
The markings correlate with Eltdown Shards. Three notebooks filling fast
Lake's journal confirms Dyer's private account. The pattern is real
The mountains of the Pnakotic texts. They're real. Everything is real
The murals confirm every text. He was right. Understanding the pattern does not make it less terrible
The texts describe what's below. He knows what they'll find. He goes anyway
Recognizes the crude spiral carvings from Eltdown Shard descriptions
The texts never said the Elder Things were men. This was not in the pattern
Tekeli-li. The word from the texts. He's been reading about this thing his entire career
Others have to help him run. The pattern is complete but it destroyed him
Barely responsive. Clutches his notebooks. Whispers correlations
The vision. He recognizes it. This was in the texts too. All of it was true
Back among people. Slowly returning to himself. The monograph he'll never publish
The Choice. His cousin died trying to warn people. Dyer was ignored. The pattern repeats. Telling or silence — neither changes what's under the ice
Not yet introduced
Available as PC — CMS nurse, recognized Dyer's descriptions from oral traditions
The briefing describes what three island cultures warned against
Departs — the old stories are warnings, not superstition
The dogs sense it. In the islands, the animals always knew first
The wind piping matches descriptions from Solomon Islands elders
These people died because they didn't listen to the warnings. As always
The forbidden mountains. Every culture has them. Every warning is the same: do not pass
The murals tell the same story the elders told. Different medium, same truth preserved across millennia
The warnings were not to prevent this. They were to prepare the survivors
Tends to others' fear. The nurse in her never stops. Compassion as armor
They were men. The oral traditions called them 'the first gardeners.' Now she understands
The Shoggoth. The thing the stories warned about. Her cross is cold against her skin
Supports Angell, who is barely functioning. The nurse endures
Tends to injuries. The practical work keeps her grounded
The vision. She prays. Not for deliverance — for understanding
Checks on everyone. Some are broken. Some are numb. All are changed
The Choice. The warnings survived millennia in human memory. The story must be told — but in a way people can carry. Not science. Story. The oral tradition continues
